title: Quiz: Regulatory Applications
description: Test your understanding of computational regulatory applications

questions.question: According to ICH M7, what is required for computational mutagenicity assessment?
questions.explanation: ICH M7 explicitly requires two complementary (Q)SAR methodologies for mutagenicity prediction - one expert rule-based system (e.g., Derek) and one statistical-based system (e.g., Sarah). If both are negative, the compound can be classified as Class 5 (not mutagenic).

questions.question: What are the OECD principles for QSAR validation? (Select the key requirement)

options.text: The model must be commercially available
The model must have defined applicability domain
The model must be approved by FDA
The model must use quantum chemistry

questions.explanation: OECD QSAR principles require: defined endpoint, unambiguous algorithm, defined applicability domain, appropriate goodness-of-fit, and mechanistic interpretation if possible. The applicability domain ensures the model is only used for compounds within its validated chemical space.

questions.question: Under REACH, when can QSAR be used instead of animal testing?

options.text: Never - animal testing is always required
When QSAR meets OECD principles and is fit for purpose

questions.explanation: REACH explicitly allows QSAR as an alternative to testing when the model meets OECD principles for QSAR validation. The prediction must be fit for purpose, the compound must be in the applicability domain, and adequate documentation must be provided.

questions.question: What is the 'weight of evidence' approach in regulatory toxicology?

options.text: Using only the most accurate single prediction
Combining multiple lines of evidence (QSAR, read-across, in vitro, etc.)

questions.explanation: Weight of evidence combines multiple lines of evidence - computational predictions, structural similarity (read-across), in vitro data, and mechanistic understanding - to reach a conclusion. This integrated approach is preferred by regulators over relying on any single source.

questions.question: Computational predictions can completely replace clinical trials for drug approval.
questions.explanation: False. While computation plays an increasing supportive role, clinical trials remain essential for drug approval. Computational methods can help design better trials and reduce preclinical attrition, but cannot replace human safety and efficacy data required for marketing authorization.

questions.question: What should be documented for each computational prediction in regulatory submissions? (Select all)
questions.explanation: All four elements are required for proper regulatory documentation: software/parameters for reproducibility, applicability domain to ensure validity, experimental validation for reliability, and limitations for transparency. Thorough documentation is essential for regulatory acceptance.
